name: implement-rate-limiter
goal:
Create a new file rate_limiter.py implementing a RateLimiter class. The class should limit function calls to a maximum number within a sliding time window. Requirements:

1. Constructor takes max_calls (int) and window_seconds (float)
2. Method is_allowed() returns True if under limit, False if over
3. Method reset() clears the call history
4. The limiter should use a sliding window (old calls outside window are not counted)
5. Must handle edge cases: exactly at limit should return True, one over should return False
implement_instructions: Create rate_limiter.py with a RateLimiter class. Use time.time() for timestamps. Store call timestamps in a list. In is_allowed(), first prune old timestamps outside the window, then check if adding a new call would exceed max_calls. If allowed, record the timestamp and return True. Be careful: at exactly max_calls it should still return True (that's the Nth allowed call), but max_calls+1 should return False.
review_instructions: Verify all tests pass. Check edge cases are handled correctly. Return FINAL_STATUS: APPROVED if all checks pass, FINAL_STATUS: CHANGES_REQUIRED if any fail.

checks.name: basic-rate-limit
checks.command: {python} -c "from rate_limiter import RateLimiter; r = RateLimiter(3, 1.0); results = [r.is_allowed() for _ in range(5)]; assert results == [True, True, True, False, False], f'Expected [T,T,T,F,F] got {results}'; print('OK')"

checks.name: window-reset
checks.command: {python} -c "import time; from rate_limiter import RateLimiter; r = RateLimiter(2, 0.1); r.is_allowed(); r.is_allowed(); assert r.is_allowed() == False, 'Should be blocked'; time.sleep(0.15); assert r.is_allowed() == True, 'Should allow after window'; print('OK')"

checks.name: manual-reset
checks.command: {python} -c "from rate_limiter import RateLimiter; r = RateLimiter(1, 10.0); r.is_allowed(); assert r.is_allowed() == False; r.reset(); assert r.is_allowed() == True, 'Should allow after reset'; print('OK')"
